cross_connections.display_label: Compiled the 1876 Talcott family genealogy of Hartford line
Compiled the 1909 Descendants of Thomas Hooker, two volumes

narrative_blocks.header: Author of the histories that document this database's
narrative_blocks.detail: After building and operating three iterations of Dwight's High School (Brooklyn 1846-1858, Clinton NY 1858-1863, New York City 1863-1867), Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight returned to Clinton NY and devoted himself to writing. His school at Brooklyn in 1846 was the first American school to offer German as a regular daily subject. As genealogical compiler he produced the definitive histories of the Dwight and Strong families — still the primary sources for both lines. He contributed to Bibliotheca Sacra, The New Englander, and the NY Genealogical Record. He briefly edited The Interior, a Presbyterian weekly in Chicago, but found it financially untenable and resigned after five months.
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin Woodbridge Dwight compiled the two-volume History of the Dwight Family and the two-volume History of the Strong Family — both primary sources for this database. His brother Theodore William Dwight founded Columbia Law School; his sister Mary Dwight married Judge Elliott Anthony of Chicago.

narrative_blocks.header: First American school to teach daily German, 1846
narrative_blocks.body: In 1846 he founded Dwight's High School in Brooklyn NY — a commercial and classical school of 120-160 boys that ran for twelve years. It is believed to be the first American school in which German was lifted to the plane of a regular daily study, taught alongside Latin and French to all pupils rather than as a dilettante elective.

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote the family genealogies, two volumes each
narrative_blocks.body: He compiled and published the History of the Descendants of John Dwight of Dedham Mass (2 vols 1874) and the History of the Descendants of Elder John Strong of Northampton (2 vols 1871) — two of the foundational New England family genealogies cited across this tree, including the Major Timothy Dwight III Natchez accounts and the Strong-Hooker double-line marriages.

Before his Brooklyn school years he founded the First Congregational Church of Joliet IL in 1844 and served as its pastor for two years. The church is now the Central Presbyterian Church of Joliet; he also briefly edited The Interior, a Presbyterian weekly out of Chicago, in 1872.

institutions.blurb: Hamilton College BA 1835; tutor at Hamilton 1839-1842; father served as Hamilton treasurer concurrently.

notable.notable_blurb: Psychiatrist; reformed asylum design at Kankakee

narrative_blocks.header: He scattered the asylum into cottages
narrative_blocks.body: As superintendent of the Kankakee State Hospital from 1879, Dewey made the “cottage plan” the basis of its design — housing patients in small homes rather than one vast institution, a model that shaped Manteno State Hospital after it. He had come to it straight from running a Prussian Army hospital in the Franco-Prussian War.

His wife Lillian died in 1880, seven years into their marriage; Dewey lived another fifty-two years without remarrying, dying in California in 1933. His memoir of pioneering American psychiatry appeared only after his death.
